data.title: ServiceTitan: Near-Term Deceleration, But Solid Long-Term Foundation
data.description:
Without a doubt, the most jarring market trend of 2026 is the speed at which enterprise software stocks have crashed to multi-year lows. In the space of just a few months, investors flipped their opinion on AI as a tailwind to business and are now fearing the displacement risk it will have across industries, particularly in enterprise SaaS companies.

ServiceTitan (TTAN), the vertical software company that addresses trades businesses (such as home technicians, electricians, and plumbers), has been among the hardest-hit companies. Since the start of the year, shares of ServiceTitan have lost more than 30% of their value, and relative to highs near $130 notched last May, the stock is down by nearly half. The key question for investors is, is ServiceTitan's decline commensurate with its fundamental performance, or is the selloff overdone?

I last wrote a "Buy" article on ServiceTitan in January, when the stock was trading just shy of $100 per share. Since then, ServiceTitan has lost ~30% of its value. Yes, there are elements that we need to be careful about here: GTV and revenue are decelerating, as ServiceTitan is being held down by a tougher macroeconomy that is sidelining many consumers from home improvements. That said, I think ServiceTitan's lower valuation, plus its consistent margin improvements, helps to compensate us for slower growth. I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating here.

It's useful for us to kick off the discussion with an overview of ServiceTitan and the possibility for AI to take over its core use cases. In short, I think it's unlikely. The chart below is a good refresher on all the core features that ServiceTitan offers to its clients. The company positions itself as a one-stop shop for tradespeople, offering marketing and CRM tools alongside scheduling, dispatch, pricing, and payment offerings.

Yes, it's true that relative to many other SaaS companies, ServiceTitan's customer base skews toward smaller, individual, or regional operators instead of large multinational firms with ingrained software systems. There is a relatively lower switching cost to ripping out ServiceTitan and building homegrown AI solutions, but is it realistic? We find it unlikely that smaller trades businesses would have the expertise, resources, or willingness to try to custom-design DIY their way around the logistical tools that ServiceTitan provides that allow them to focus on their customers. And in many cases, ServiceTitan's technology leads to positive ROI for its customers: in case studies, ServiceTitan cites customers who have been able to reduce staffing levels and boost EBITDA margins due to the company's more efficient dispatching systems.

We note as well that ServiceTitan is also achieving a larger proportion of usage revenue, tying its revenue less to seats and customer counts and allowing the company more room to achieve long-term growth as its customers' reliance on the platform increases. As a reminder, ServiceTitan believes its TAM to be $30+ billion, inclusive of sub-verticals the company currently doesn't service (like heavy construction) - so despite its niche nature, we note that ServiceTitan's annual revenue of just over $1 billion is only about 3%-4% penetration into the overall TAM.

The core concerns that we need to consider are mostly macro-related. High-interest rates and a persistently shaky macro are dampening the housing market and also discouraging home improvements. That said, we also think that we have fair compensation from a valuation perspective to balance out these near-term risks.

At current share prices near $69, ServiceTitan trades at a $6.50 billion market cap. Netting off the $428.8 million of cash (which is now free of debt) on the company's latest balance sheet, ServiceTitan's resulting enterprise value is $6.07 billion.

For FY27 (the year for ServiceTitan ending in January 2027), the company is guiding to $1.11-$1.12 billion in revenue, or 16%-17% y/y growth.

This positions ServiceTitan's valuation at 5.4x EV/FY27 revenue. While ServiceTitan certainly isn't the biggest value deal available in the enterprise software industry at the moment, we note that the company is sitting either in line or cheaper against a number of other mid/high teens revenue growth stocks:

Stay long here and use this year's sharp slide as a buying opportunity.

Q4 Download

Let's now go through ServiceTitan's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

ServiceTitan's revenue grew 21% y/y to $254.0 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $245.5 million (+17% y/y) by a four-point margin. While the beat is impressive, we note that growth did decelerate four points as well versus 21% y/y growth in Q3. Meanwhile, underlying subscription revenue of $192 million grew at a slightly faster 23% y/y pace.

Gross transaction value, which represents the total dollar value of all transactions conducted through ServiceTitan's platform, also showed a substantial six-point deceleration to 16% y/y growth, from 22% y/y growth in Q3.

A larger portion of ServiceTitan's revenue is shifting to usage-based deals. Usage-based revenue grew 22% y/y to $53 million in the quarter, representing 0.27% of GTV (slightly higher y/y). The company continues to bank on usage revenue becoming a larger slice of the pie and a key growth driver going forward. Per CFO Dave Sherry's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

Usage revenue grew 22% year-over-year to $53 million. FinTech utilization remained strong again this period. We also benefited from monetization of our partner ecosystem, which does not directly correlate with GTV and from early growth in Virtual Agents revenue. Looking forward, we believe that growth from these factors could lead Usage revenue to grow more quickly than GTV in FY '27. Total platform revenue for Q4, the sum of Subscription and Usage revenue grew 23% year-over-year to $245 million.

Another positive indicator: net revenue retention rates clocked in at 110% in Q4, indicating that the average customer expanded their spending on ServiceTitan's platform by 10% y/y, net of churning customers. This signals that customers are willing to continue building with ServiceTitan for the long haul and defrays any notion that ServiceTitan can be easily replaced by AI.

On the profitability front, ServiceTitan notes that the larger, higher-spending customer base drove a 360 bps y/y lift in pro forma gross margins to 73.8% (a chunk of the y/y lift was also driven by the company reclassifying certain expenses to the sales and marketing line). Meanwhile, pro forma operating margins rose at a stunning 740 bps y/y pace to 10.7%, from just 3.3% in the year-ago Q4.

Key Takeaways

We, of course, need to be wary of the fact that ServiceTitan is running up against a cooler macroeconomic climate that may be sapping demand for home improvement projects. This is readily observed in the company's slowing revenue and GTV growth rates. At the same time, however, the sudden and sharp crash in the stock overstates what is largely a cynical problem for ServiceTitan. Looking longer term, we think the company is still building a fantastic foundation, with large improvements in operating margin and a shift to capturing more customer spend via usage-based deals.

Take advantage of this dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: NORW: Profiting From The Energy Crisis, But Risk-Reward Isn't Ideal (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Background

The Global X MSCI Norway ETF (NORW), which has been in existence since 2010 (with AUM aggregating to over $130M) is a passively managed ETF that tracks the MSCI Norway IMI 25/50 Index (which, in turn, is set-up to cover 99% of the entire free-float of the Norwegian equity markets). As things stand, NORW currently covers 56 stocks in total with the largest stake of the portfolio (45%) consisting of Norwegian mid-caps.

Over the last four years, we have written about this product a couple of times before but only turned bullish on it in January 2025. Our more optimistic stance 14 months ago, was driven by a more salubrious macro back drop, cheap valuations, a compelling dividend landscape, and a promising technical backdrop. NORW, has since done pretty well, generating total returns of ~54%, which is almost twice as much as its European peers, and 2.7x more than global stocks.

After a relatively rewarding 14 months, how should investors approach NORW now?

The Ongoing Energy Crisis Bodes Well, But Rate Hikes Could Also Be On The Cards

Norway’s macro picture still looks pretty encouraging. Compared to the tepid real GDP growth (0.6-0.7% p.a) that was seen in 2023/2024, the growth trajectory is now more than double that pace at around the 1.7% levels.

We suspect there could be upside risks to GDP growth this year (potentially closer to 2%), as NORW’s strong export positioning has received another fillip of late. Compared to last year, where exports served as a drag on growth (-1.8%), this year, things were poised to pick up (by 1%), even before the Iran war reared its ugly head. Now however, with restrictions around the Strait of Hormuz, there are signs of a brewing energy crisis in Europe; Norway, which is one of the most energy-rich nations that exports around 88% of its total energy production stands to gain. For further context, note that Norway currently meets 20% of Europe’s oil demand and 30% of its natural gas demand.

Interestingly, the Norwegian energy major – Equinor ASA (EQNR), which is incidentally also the largest holding in NORW’s portfolio (with a weight of over 13%) is currently operating at full capacity, and is well poised to charge premiums on its production (EQNR serves as the largest pipe gas exporter in Europe), which typically works out to around $2 per MMBtu). Think of the margins that could potentially be made across the quarter, when EU prices as of late January were already trending higher at around $12/MMBtu.

Having said that, everything may not necessarily be hunky dory for Norway, as one should also keep in mind the risk of import inflation, and how that could dictate the central bank’s monetary policy decisions. One of the reasons why NORW has done pretty well, is that the Norges Bank has been driving easier monetary conditions with 50bps worth of rate cuts seen since mid-2025 with the rate now at 4%.

However, core inflation over the last couple of months has already been catching the central bank off guard, and it is now expected that rates could well end up back at the 4.5% level by the end of this year.

Dividend Picture Is Not As Stellar As Before

Another supplementary reason for pursuing NORW back in January 2025, was because its yield position looked very alluring. To highlight, while European ETFs (such as the iShares Core MSCI Europe ETF (IEUR)) were yielding decent enough figures of over 3% back then, NORW was offering a yield that was closer to 6%! That narrative has now flipped, with NORW only offering a yield of less than 2.8% (on par with the median ETF yield), which is not only below what IEUR is offering (still maintaining 3% levels), but also a good 100bps below its own historical standards (over the past five years, it has averaged 3.8%).

Admittedly, some of this is a function of the meaningful price appreciation we’ve seen, but also consider that last year’s payouts were quite underwhelming compared to what was seen in 2023 and 2024 (over a 3-year basis, annual dividends have not grown, and relative to last year alone, it has contracted by 27%)

Looking ahead, it is difficult to imagine those yields getting back up to those 6% levels any time soon, and we’re staring at quite a mixed dividend outlook, considering what the top holdings of NORW have in store.

For instance, while NORW’s largest holding- Equinor, saw its most recent dividend hike only come in at 5% YoY, but the company’s ambition is to typically grow their dividends in line with their underlying earnings, and consensus believes that the company could see around 18% earnings growth by the end of this year.

Meanwhile, EQNR is also going to trim its organic CAPEX by $4B over the next couple of years, which should provide further support to free cash flow and the scope of diverting that to shareholder distributions (this may not necessarily go towards dividends and could come via repurchases).

In contrast, the only other large holding with a double-digit percentage weight in NORW- DNB Bank ASA (DNBBY), which has a policy of paying back 50% of its profits, is staring at lower earnings of around 6% YoY, which won’t reflect well on the distributions.

All in all, it's fair to say that, while it certainly isn’t an issue, NORW’s dividend profile is not as attractive as before.

Solid Momentum, But Risk-Reward Looks Unfavorable

Considering the ongoing conditions in the global energy markets, investors won’t be surprised to note that NORW is also seeing very strong momentum of late. On the short-term daily charts, since November, the ETF has seen a pattern of higher-highs and higher-lows and is now trading above all its key moving averages (also a good 20% above its 200DMA). While this set-up may enthuse momentum players, those who like to take a broader perspective of things, ought to pay attention to what the long-term charts are suggesting.

The last time we wrote about NORW, we viewed it as an ideal candidate to attract additional European fund flows, as its relative strength ratio (versus other European markets) was trading at a deep discount to its long-term mean. In the following months, we’ve seen that upward mean reversion trade play out, and now the set-up doesn’t look very encouraging with the current ratio, perched at an 8% premium over its long-term average. Also, the valuation gap between Norwegian stocks and European stocks isn’t as wide as it was back then when NORW was priced at just a 10x earnings multiple. As thing sand, NORW is now priced at over 14x earnings and 1.4x sales, which is not an ocean away from the corresponding multiples of European stocks ( 15.9x earnings and 1.6x sales).

Meanwhile, if we look at NORW’s own long-term charts (the monthly time frame), there are quite a few signs of it coming across as overextended to the upside. Firstly, over the last 14 years, we can see that NORW has progressed higher, but respected the boundaries of an ascending channel (the two black lines). It is now perched right around the upper channel boundary. Secondly, pretty much the entire candle of March 2026 is now priced outside the upper bollinger band (which is already two standard deviations away from the 20-month rolling average). Thirdly, the RSI, indicator has reached overbought levels, which were last seen in 2021, after which there wasn’t a meaningful upward move in the price.

Closing Thoughts

NORW has experienced a solid run since we last covered it, and it still continues to see strong momentum, because of Norway’s strong positioning in the energy export markets. However, conditions on the long-term charts look overextended, and the dividend profile isn’t as alluring as before. We thus think it would be sensible to take some profits now and revert our rating to a hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: HeartFlow: Getting Into The Flow
data.description:
Shares of HeartFlow, Inc. (HTFL) have recovered to levels in the mid-twenties following a solid end to the year 2025, as the company provided guidance for 2026 that pleased investors.

While growth rates are set to come down dramatically, the market seems to read the guidance as a conservative one. Investors are pleased about the adoption of new application groups, as well as prospects for gross margin gains, having the potential to deliver on greater operating leverage down the road.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including the areas of technology and medical applications, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

Maintaining Growth

HearthFlow reported both full-year and fourth quarter sales growth of 40% for the year 2025. This means that full-year sales surpassed $176 million, as fourth-quarter revenues of $49 million reveal a run rate near $200 million.

The company reported a full-year operating loss of $64 million, up minimally from the year before, indicating some relative progress on the margin front. The same goes for fourth-quarter losses of $17.5 million, trending around $70 million each year, equal to about 35% of sales. Adjusted metrics, such as an adjusted EBITDA loss of $44.7 million, improved by a million compared to 2024.

These revenues were generated from an installed base of 1,465 accounts after 340 accounts were added during the year. For the year 2026, the company guided for about 25% growth, seeing revenues to come in between $218 and $222 million. No overall margin guidance has been provided, apart from gross margins, which are seen at 80-81%, compared to 77% reported in 2025. While the losses likely remain substantial, fortunately, the company has a net cash position of around $280 million, as I would expect losses to come in a bit in the coming year.

With some 85 million shares outstanding, these shares trading at $25, the market value of the firm stands at just over $2.1 billion, or just under $1.9 billion if we factor in net cash holdings. This results in a forward sales multiple of 8-9 times for the operating assets.

Some Perspective

HearthFlow focuses on helping to avoid heart-related diseases and deaths, as heart disease is the prevalent cause of death in the US. The product and solution are based on CAD testing, which is a non-invasive test, one that avoids unnecessary testing while helping to improve medical management.

Its 3D model provides actionable insights into plaque volumes, blood flows, and stenosis to overcome the limitations of other non-invasive tests, often giving false positives or negatives. This should provide the new standard of care versus traditional CAD, with the AI platform allowing for detection, diagnosis, management, and treatment.

The company went public at $19 in August, at the time growing sales by around 40% as well, and that has been the real growth cadence throughout 2025. This was aided by some fundamental support, with the FDA clearing its next-generation plaque analysis algorithm in September. With the company actually ending the year with a similar percentage growth rate, I feel comforted that the 2026 guidance might be on the conservative side.

Hints about this might have been indicated on the conference call, with the company calling for first-quarter growth exceeding 30%, with 1-2% sequential growth seen thereafter. The second quarter is considered a bit softer, thanks to seasonal trends, yet more growth should be seen in the second half of the year, as the plague business will see a bigger contribution.

That would be welcome news, with shares now trading off the highs around the $40 mark, as current losses can be maintained for years, even if no absolute decline in operating losses is seen just yet. This might happen in 2026, given a 3-4% anticipated increase in gross margins and leverage throughout the organization.

Large Growth Opportunities

Sales growth anticipated in 2026 is seen at around 25%, with total revenues seen at a midpoint of around $220 million, including a $16 million contribution from plaque revenues, as the 3-4% increase in gross margins would help the bottom line by some $7 million. Moreover, the company upped the mid-term gross margins target by five points to 85% of sales, which helps in creating a roadmap to see operating leverage, even in gross margins.

The company focuses heavily on improving the analysis and innovation part, using AI actively to use its proprietary database, containing well over 160 million CT images. These are ground truth and non-public domain data, making it hard for AI to find its way around that, effectively shielding the business from AI, at least that is what the company claims.

Beyond growth in these core categories, the company sees potential for the technology (or derivatives thereof) to expand into other areas as well. Moreover, underlying traction is even stronger than reported revenue growth, with global cases up 53% to nearly 58,000 in the fourth quarter, all suggesting stronger traction than reported sales growth.

In my view, the company continues to take market share from market incumbents. While the innovative qualities are seen, the softer growth for 2026 doesn't necessarily make me become a buyer yet, even if some margin gains are seen.

I am still performing a balancing act, seeing the potential of disruptive technology. However, still elevated sales multiples, a slower pace of growth, and substantial losses make me a bit cautious here, awaiting more clues as the year progresses.

data.title: Adobe: Bears Are Pricing In Disruption While Overlooking The Monetization Shift
data.description:
The Market Disconnect

Adobe's (ADBE) stock has declined by more than 60% from its 2024 peak, driving the valuation down to roughly 14x forward earnings -- the lowest multiple in over a decade. This is in spite of revenue still growing by over 10% annually, strong and improving margins, and over $10 billion in TTM free cash flow.

Rather than weakening fundamentals, the poor stock performance is largely due to three primary investor concerns:

  1. Canva and other lower-cost tools will steal market share.
  2. Generative AI (GenAI) will commoditize high-end creative software, degrading Adobe's pricing power.
  3. Seat compression due to AI productivity gains will cripple Adobe's subscription model.

After evaluating these concerns, I believe the market is seriously discounting Adobe's competitive positioning. I also believe the market has fundamentally mistaken Adobe's business model in the AI era by pricing in disruption without accounting for increased monetization potential.

Canva vs. Adobe: Why Canva Still Has a Long Way to Go

Canva's mission is to democratize creative software. The company has been extremely successful in this endeavor -- particularly among non-professionals and hobbyists -- as evidenced by their 265+ million global MAUs and 31+ million paid users.

While speed and simplicity are Canva's core strengths, they also create a ceiling to the functionality and customization the platform can offer. It is not very feasible for complex, original work, which is Adobe's core value proposition. Canva has been making moves upmarket with the release of Canva Enterprise and their acquisition of Affinity, an enterprise-grade Adobe competitor that wraps individual products similar to Illustrator, Photoshop, and InDesign into a single application. However, while Canva is trying to make headway in the enterprise market, Adobe has been deepening its already tight grip on this customer base, making it much more difficult for Canva to compete in the enterprise arena.

  • Between FY23 and FY25, more than 20 of Adobe's top 50 enterprise customers doubled their ARR spend on Adobe products.
  • Adobe surpassed 150 total customers spending more than $10 million in ARR in FY25 and grew this number by 20% year-over-year in Q1 FY26.
  • By early FY25, Adobe had ~22,000 enterprise customers, around 1,500 of whom use 5+ products.

Adobe also made moves to retain their lower-end customers that Canva has been going after with Adobe Express -- an all-in-one, AI-powered content creation app. With its "freemium" model, Express is essentially an "on ramp" that allows students and non-professionals to become familiar with the products for free and then move into the full Creative Cloud as their career progresses.

Express's paid tier is also cheaper than Canva, with Canva Pro costing $15 per month per person and Adobe Express Premium costing only $9.99 per month per person. Because Express integrates directly with Creative Cloud and the rest of Adobe's core infrastructure, it can help retain users as they transition from hobbyist to professional work -- something Canva may find difficult to replicate.

Canva is excellent for quick content creation. Adobe is the infrastructure enabling multinational corporations to manage thousands of assets across departments while also providing legal indemnification. This is a gap that Canva has yet to fill.

While Canva has been stealing some lower-value customers, Adobe still maintains a dominant ~70% share of the graphics software market:

  • Adobe Photoshop (37.2%)
  • Adobe InDesign (21.8%)
  • Canva (20.6%)
  • Adobe Illustrator (10.5%)

Given this market dynamic and Adobe's complete content infrastructure supporting the full creative workflow, Canva still has a long way to go, particularly in the enterprise market.

While Canva is one of the most cited competitive threats, the biggest drag on Adobe's valuation has been the AI disruption narrative.

The Two-Pronged AI Bear Case

Likely the most pervasive narrative weighing on Adobe's stock is the double threat of AI.

The Homogenization Effect

The first component of the AI narrative is that the ease of creating high-quality content using AI tools will eliminate the need to learn Adobe's complex software. While worth consideration, this idea overlooks the purpose of enterprise content creation.

For visual advertising, the primary goal is to generate brand awareness and appeal that separates the enterprises from competitors. Currently, AI tools generate approximations of their training data contextualized based on a text prompt. If all companies cut creative teams in favor of AI-first strategies, the result would be a largely homogenized content landscape with minimal differentiation, which goes directly against the very purpose of creating ad campaigns.

Research on this homogenization effect has shown that even before model retraining, the content produced when used repeatedly and without human mediation is already generic. Furthermore, as the amount of AI-generated content grows, this data will be used in the retraining process, not only amplifying this homogenization but also leading to diminishing output quality.

AI Productivity Gains Could Actually Grow Subscriptions

The second major component of the AI disruption narrative is the idea that massive productivity gains unlocked using AI will enable fewer employees to get more done, potentially leading to smaller creative teams and eroding subscriptions. Paradoxically, however, I anticipate we will see either unchanged or growing subscriptions fueled by these productivity gains.

As AI drastically increases the amount of content that can be created, the unit cost per output is reduced. Moreover, this increased amount of content will enable enterprises to shift from a few broad ad campaigns to a variety of hyper-personalized versions, boosting engagement.

We've already seen this with IBM (IBM), which tested Adobe Firefly by generating 200 images with 1,000+ variations for an ad campaign. The Firefly-generated campaign yielded 26x more engagement than the benchmark, and 20% of engagements came from C-level managers. These results suggest that Adobe's AI tools can generate hyper-personalized content that boosts engagement while also increasing ROI through targeting higher-value audiences.

The demand for hyper-personalized content is seemingly endless, so producing more of this content with the help of AI should boost engagement and lift ROI. When enterprises competing in the "attention" market see rising creative ROI, the logical next step is to reinvest in creative teams to maximize output of this high-yielding content.

When discussing the study, Billy Seabrook, global chief design officer for IBM Consulting, explained how Firefly has streamlined their creative workflow and enabled their professional designers to focus less on redundant tasks and more on higher-value work like concepting and ideation. It seems like enterprises are using AI not to replace creatives, but rather to unlock their full potential.

Adobe's Shift from "Seats" to "Seats + Usage"

Additionally, while AI reduces the human workload, they do not replace the tools used to perform the work. As enterprises scale their AI use, the usage of these software tools will also increase. Adobe has experienced this first-hand with their AI offerings driving both new user growth and increasing usage of their tools. This is why the company augmented their seat-based model with a usage-based component.

Adobe subscriptions include a set number of "generative credits" per month. Every time an AI agent calls an Adobe API, credits are consumed, with the number of credits consumed scaling with the computational intensity of the task. As of the end of Q1FY26, the addition of the usage-based generative credit model has been a source of reinvigorating growth:

  • Generative credit consumption grew over 45% quarter-over-quarter.
  • Firefly's ARR grew 75% quarter-over-quarter to over $250 million.
  • AI-first ARR more than tripled year-over-year.

If credit consumption continues its rapid growth, we could be looking at a multi-billion-dollar revenue stream, and as enterprises scale their agents toward around-the-clock production, we could potentially see this play out, even if seat growth stagnates.

The "Clean" AI Differentiator

One of Adobe's most overlooked competitive advantages that I believe will continue driving AI revenue growth pertains to the data used to train their AI models. Most leading image-generating models (e.g., DALL-E, Stable Diffusion, and Midjourney) were trained, at least to an extent, on unlicensed data. Training models on unlicensed data has led to some major copyright suits, most notably the New York Times v. OpenAI case.

The Times alleges that OpenAI and Microsoft (MSFT) were providing unauthorized reproductions and derivations of their copyrighted content to users for free, compromising their business model. If the courts rule in favor of The Times, OpenAI would likely have to pay billions of dollars in penalties.

Understanding these implications, Adobe chose to prioritize enterprise safety. The company trained its Firefly models exclusively on licensed content, stock images, and public domain data, enabling it to offer copyright/IP indemnification to enterprise customers. While a few AI companies have subsequently implemented some form of indemnification, Adobe is one of the very few that developed their models specifically to eliminate copyright/IP risk entirely.

Corporate legal teams don't just prefer this type of safety when using AI for commercial purposes; they often require it. This approach to AI training doesn't just give Adobe an edge over the competition; it actively disqualifies many competing models. This gives Adobe the advantage it needs to continue growing credit consumption and turn the usage-based revenue supplement into a multi-billion-dollar income stream.

The Moat Is Wider Than It Seems

While each of the concerns detailed above are valid, Adobe's competitive advantages remain intact and underappreciated despite successfully protecting the company from numerous past threats.

The Quicksand Effect

Firstly, Adobe's proprietary file formats (.pdf, .ai, .psd, etc.) make switching vendors not only difficult but risky. While other apps can import these formats, it is rarely done so with 100% accuracy, so migrating these assets to a competitor comes with a substantial risk of file incompatibility and makes editing past work significantly more difficult.

Additionally, Adobe's software has the reputation of being extremely complex, with mastery often taking years. If an enterprise with hundreds of designers switched to new software, they are not just paying for the new licenses; they are paying for the thousands of hours of lost productivity while staff learn the intricacies of the new software, making switching not only inconvenient but also expensive.

Furthermore, when taking a broader view of an enterprise's overall advertising budget, Adobe subscriptions represent a relatively minute piece of the pie. The average annual salary for digital graphic designers is ~$62,500, while one Creative Cloud Pro Plus license costs roughly $1,325 per year. When adding in costs for ad placement and engagement, Adobe licenses amount to less than 2% of the overall budget. With everything that Adobe's software enables a designer to accomplish, the minimal relative savings from switching vendors simply do not justify the costs of productivity loss and risks associated with asset migration.

Lastly, Adobe's shift from selling apps to selling the entire content supply chain has made it even harder for customers to rationalize switching vendors. With products like GenStudio, Adobe has now integrated project management with content creation and distribution. For large enterprise customers, Adobe has transformed from a photo editor to the mission-critical infrastructure supporting their entire content workflows.

This transition to selling the entire content supply chain has also created a supplemental data moat. Because Adobe manages the entire supply chain, an enterprise may have decades worth of workflow and user behavior data, asset libraries, and brand guidelines stored within Adobe's ecosystem. Not only would this make asset migration an even bigger headache, but it also gives Adobe's AI offerings an edge. Enterprises can train custom Firefly models using their own proprietary data stored within Adobe's ecosystem to generate content within their brand parameters. When AI training data increasingly emerges as a competitive differentiator, this gives Adobe a prominent advantage.

"Industry Standard" Status is Self-Reinforcing

Adobe's industry dominance isn't just protected by switching costs; its status as the "professional standard" forms a self-reinforcing feedback loop. Because Adobe's tools were far superior to anything else in the 1990s and 2000s, they became essential for creative teams, making their software the industry standard, a position they still hold today.

As previously mentioned, Adobe remains the dominant platform within the graphics software market, with two products individually holding greater shares of the market than Canva's entire product suite. Because Adobe products are so ubiquitous, they are often considered a baseline requirement for building a career in the creative field. This has incentivized more than 100 universities to become "Adobe Creative Campuses" that provide all students and faculty with full Creative Cloud and Express access.

The "Creative Campus" designation makes these universities more desirable because graduates trained on Adobe often have more employment opportunities and earn higher pay.

As more graduates are trained using Adobe software, enterprises are further incentivized to keep Adobe so as to not limit their hiring pools. This minor network effect has enabled Adobe to continue onboarding new users who then become locked in by the switching costs.

The switching costs and network effect have created a quicksand dynamic -- the longer an enterprise is in Adobe's ecosystem, the more difficult it becomes to leave.

The Fundamentals Tell a Different Story

There is one major problem with the overall disruption narrative: the fundamentals don't support it.

Adobe has sustained revenue growth above 10% over the last half-decade, and Q1FY26 showed signs of reacceleration. This past quarter, Adobe posted year-over-year revenue growth of 12%, the largest since FY22. This reacceleration is made better by the company's ability to maintain an impressive margin profile.

Adobe's ability to expand margins, particularly during times of increased AI investment, demonstrates the impressive operating leverage of the business. Even if revenue growth slows to the high single digits, Adobe will likely still be able to achieve double-digit profitability growth going forward.

If Adobe were being disrupted across creative professions, we'd expect to see pronounced deterioration of subscription revenues from creative and marketing professionals, but this is not the case. In the most recent quarter, year-over-year subscription revenue growth from this customer group actually reached its highest level since customer segment reporting began.

It is worth mentioning that management's Q2 FY26 revenue guidance for the creative and marketing professionals customer group is between $4.41 billion and $4.44 billion -- roughly 10% YoY growth. However, Adobe has exceeded revenue guidance for 23 consecutive quarters by an average of 1.2% at the midpoint. With this history of issuing conservative guidance and the growing adoption of Adobe's AI tools, I anticipate revenue growth in this customer group will again exceed guidance by a similar margin.

Adobe's annual recurring revenue (ARR) metrics are also not indicative of a company being meaningfully disrupted. Total ARR at the end of the most recent quarter reached $26.1 billion (+10.9% YoY), and management is guiding for another year of double-digit growth in FY26.

Additionally, Adobe has demonstrated that it is still a cash-generating machine. In the trailing 12-month period, the business generated $10.6 billion in operating cash flow and $10.3 billion in free cash flow with an FCF margin above 42%. Because SaaS companies generally incur significant non-cash expenses in the form of stock-based compensation (SBC), it is important to account for its dilutive effects on cash flows. When adjusting for SBC, Adobe still generated $8.34 billion in free cash flow, or $19.91 per share.

Finally, Adobe is one of the more efficiently run corporations in the market. In FY25, the company earned an ROIC of 36% -- notably higher than the 29.3% industry average -- marking the ninth consecutive fiscal year achieving an ROIC above 20%.

If Adobe's market position were truly eroding -- especially to the degree that the market is pricing in -- it would be blatantly obvious in the fundamentals. As of right now, this is not so evident.

How Cheap Is Adobe Stock?

As of the writing of this article, Adobe trades at around $246/share, down more than 60% from the 2024 highs. During this decline, Adobe's P/E and P/FCF multiples contracted materially from ~54x and ~38x, respectively, to current levels of ~14x and ~10x, respectively. This multiple compression shown below has largely been reflective of the market's fear that AI and other tools are going to materially disrupt Adobe's business.

While, optically, Adobe shares are severely undervalued, I also performed a DCF analysis to confirm this implied undervaluation. Revenue and EBIT margin assumptions for each case are shown in the following graphic:

The bear case assumes that revenue growth slows to 3% and EBIT margins decline to 33.5% over the next five years due to AI disruption and competition impacting Adobe's pricing power and seat growth. The base case assumes relatively stable revenue growth in the 9-10% range with margins remaining around 35% over the next five years due to moderate growth in Creative Cloud and Firefly adoption. The bull case assumes AI adoption materially increases enterprise content creation, allowing Adobe to accelerate revenue growth to 11.5% over the next five years with EBIT margins of 37%.

Because Adobe has used up almost all of its share repurchase authorization, the model assumes the diluted share count will remain constant. The model applies a 2.5% terminal growth rate, in line with historical U.S. GDP growth. The current yield on 10-year U.S. treasuries (4.23%) was used for the risk-free rate, and the average equity risk premium since 1960 (4.25%) was used for the market risk premium. For simplicity, the same discount rate was used for all three scenarios, but a scenario-specific approach would reduce the implied upside of the bear case.

Using these assumptions, we get the following implied intrinsic values:

Given the evidence presented, I believe the impending threats from Canva and GenAI are being overestimated, and, in the event Adobe is supplanted, it will be a slow burn rather than a rapid customer exodus. I also anticipate Adobe's AI tools, like Firefly, will continue gaining enterprise adoption. Based on this analysis, I believe the intrinsic value of Adobe stock is somewhere between the base case and the bull case -- likely in the $325-335 range. This price range implies a current upside of 32-36%, which is enough to warrant a 'buy' rating for Adobe shares.

Where Things Could Still Go Wrong

While I have spent most of this article arguing against the prevailing narratives surrounding Adobe's disruption, this does not mean that risks are nonexistent.

The "Canva Generation"

While Canva's tools are far from matching Adobe's all-encompassing, mission-critical content ecosystem, they are making significant headway, particularly with adoption among younger generations. Just as most seasoned designers and marketing managers grew up on Adobe's product suite, designating Adobe as the industry standard, Gen Z is growing up on Canva. Right now, designers and content managers use Adobe not only for its quality but also because most employers and clients require it. If Adobe's strategy of integrating its products into the education system becomes weakened with a growing preference for Canva, and if Adobe Express fails in its "on ramp" function for hobbyists and prosumers, Adobe's status as the industry standard, particularly among enterprises, could be in jeopardy.

If an entire generation has foregone Adobe's suite for Canva, this seriously limits the hiring pool for employers in need of creative professionals, potentially leading them to make the shift to Canva and supplanting Adobe as the standard. Additionally, firms could conclude that Canva is "good enough" for a majority of social advertisements, amplifying the threat. While it would likely take at least a decade or more to play out, this slow burn could lead to serious struggles for Adobe in the future.

Walking a Tightrope with Generative Credit Pricing

Though exaggerated, the bearish AI narrative does contain a bit of tangible risk. While early results are promising, Adobe's generative credit pricing strategy needs to be proven at scale. Management is walking a tightrope. They need to be able to charge enough for generative credits to produce meaningful growth without alienating mid-tier customers. This could be a difficult task that, if not executed well, could spell problems for the company's usage-based revenue going forward.

The Figma Debacle

Adobe was willing to drastically overpay for Figma before the deal was called off at the end of CY23. While Adobe has demonstrated they remain capable of strong organic growth with Firefly and other AI initiatives, the price Adobe was willing to pay for Figma could suggest Adobe is lacking confidence in outcompeting rivals with their design tools. If this perceived lack of confidence is the reality, it could be a sign that Adobe is struggling to find ways to improve their tools and keep up with competition. With regulators demonstrating increased scrutiny toward any large future acquisitions, the company now has to rely more heavily on organic growth, which is typically slower with less predictable results than M&A.

The Bottom Line

Adobe is not in the process of being disrupted. It's simply in the process of transitioning from a seat-based model to a "seats + usage" model. Because Adobe's generative credits, for the most part, require a subscription, the company is benefiting from the stability of their subscription model while also gaining from the higher growth potential of the consumption-based model, enabling the company to better monetize its customer base. As a company primarily focused on large enterprises with high consumption capacity, I believe we will continue seeing revenue growth reaccelerate, leading to an upward valuation reset. The market has priced in the risks associated with the old model, but it hasn't yet priced in the potential of the new one. This is why I believe Adobe stock is a buy at current levels.

data.title: Geron: Minimal Sales Growth But A Potential Run-Up Ahead
data.description:
Geron Corporation (GERN) could have multiple clinical readouts in 2026, serving as a potential catalyst beyond how the company performs commercially with Rytelo (imetelstat). When I last wrote about GERN in January, I rated the name a buy, based on the company's statements suggesting it was under-promising and could thus overdeliver on 2026 Rytelo sales performance. This article looks at the updated sales numbers, insights from recent company presentations, and clinical readouts ahead.

A miss on revenues

With Q4 '25 earnings on February 25, 2026, GERN reported Q4 '25 net product revenues of $48M and total revenues of $48M, missing analyst estimates by $2.41M. Net product revenues were $183.6M for 2025, which contrasts with GERN's guidance for 2026 for net product revenues of $220M-$240M, which would require 25% growth at the midpoint of that guidance. GERN's Q4 '25 earnings press release and February corporate presentation refer to Rytelo demand growing by 9% when comparing Q4 '25 to Q3 '25. The actual revenue growth, however, might be closer to 1.8%, with Q3 '25 net product revenues being $47.17M. Notably, GERN reported Q4 '24 net product revenues of $47.5M, so there hasn't been much growth to speak of from Q4 '24 to Q4 '25.

Underlying demand growth of 9%, which comes from "Geron internal data on file" is modest. That being said, GERN's CEO, who would have a picture of these demand trends over time, seemed to believe this was meaningful on the road to GERN achieving its 2026 guidance. The CEO's comments regarding Q1 '26 represent the most important hint from the Q4 '25 earnings call, in my opinion.

I would say the Q4 demand growth of 9% is an important metric for us because it is forward-looking, and that's something we always look at. From what we see, obviously, we're not going to comment on Q1, but we -- what we have seen from IQVIA and others is in line with our expectation. That's why we're reiterating guidance of growth of a top line between $220 million and $240 million.

Harout Semerjian, CEO, President & Director at GERN, Q4 '25 earnings call. Emphasis mine.

Another promising metric might be an increase in ordering accounts, since Rytelo does work for some patients (40% achieved transfusion independence of eight or more weeks in IMerge). As such, many that try the drug will see it work in their patients, and those physicians seem particularly likely to use the drug again.

As for the usage of Rytelo in first- and second-line therapy, the company had reported with Q3 '25 earnings in November that they had achieved a number of 36% for that quarter.

During the quarter, demand for RYTELO was down 3% compared to last quarter. New patient starts in the first and second line increased to 36% compared to 30% in Q2.

Harout Semerjian, CEO, President & Director at GERN, Q3 '25 earnings call.

GERN's current corporate presentation notes ~30% of new patient starts on Rytelo are in first- and second-line patients, although that number now seems to represent a rolling 12-month basis ending in October 2025. As such, the new metric doesn't really allow a direct comparison to previous numbers and doesn't incorporate much Q4 '25 data anyway.

Additional shots on goal in the clinic

GERN has also begun speaking more about investigator-sponsored trials (ISTs) of imetelstat. I find this update to be particularly positive, since an IST can represent a cheap way to further explore the efficacy of a drug, including in new indications (GERN would provide imetelstat, but other costs of the trial are covered by GERN).

We also expanded our engagement with academic centers to support the high interest in imetelstat to initiate more ISTs, and we are also seeing increased interest in community centers wanting to contribute to preclinical, clinical and real-world evidence data generation.

We have aligned to support over 10 ISTs and real-world evidence spanning mechanistic studies, combination and sequencing, early line use and new settings. We are seeing increasing interest from both academic and community centers to participate in evidence generation, and we expect initial real-world evidence data to be available in the second half of 2026.

Joseph Eid, EVP of R&D and CMO at GERN, Q4'25 earnings call. Emphasis mine.

While many of these ISTs might only produce data in a handful or tens of patients, they could provide confidence that a subsequent pivotal trial in a new indication might succeed. The ISTs, then, represent a potential step on the road to label expansion. While initial data in H2 '26 is referred to as real-world evidence, such evidence could aid marketing of Rytelo in the current indication. Further, data from ISTs in other indications could provide another opportunity for GERN to demonstrate that Rytelo can go beyond its current indication. Of course the interim analysis in IMpactMF represents the major readout, given the size of the trial, the overall survival endpoint.

The registry Clinicaltrials.gov lists 46 studies for imetelstat, although only six are "recruiting" or "active, not recruiting" at the time of writing. Some of these trials include IMerge (still ongoing despite reading out years ago and leading to approval of imetelstat) or IMpactMF. There are three studies listed not sponsored by GERN, including the IMAGINE trial in relapsed acute myeloid leukemia (AML), the IMpress trial in high-risk myelodysplastic syndromes or AML, and a trial run by the Children's Oncology Group of imetelstat in AML, MDS, or juvenile myelomonocytic leukemia (JMML).

Interim results from the IMpress study were seen at the American Society of Hematology Conference in 2025, although GERN's December 2025 press release summarizing the results notes limited single-agent activity. To be fair, that trial is enrolling patients that have already had or can't tolerate hypomethylating agents. A poster of the results is available, which has data with cutoffs of May and June 2025, so an H2 '26 update could provide another year of data, although it isn't clear if this is on tap.

Financial Overview

GERN's $48M in net product revenue for Q4 '25 doesn't bring the company to profitability, with the company still reporting a net loss of $31.1M for Q4 '25. Total costs and operating expenses were $75.8M in Q4 '25, with cost of goods sold at $1.3M, R&D expense at $15.8M, and SG&A at $41.7M. There were also restructuring charges of $17M in Q4 '25, although GERN's CFO did note that substantially all expenses associated with that were accounted for in Q4 '25.

GERN finished Q4 '25 with cash, cash equivalents, restricted cash and marketable securities of $401M, although the company does have $119.5M of noncurrent debt on its balance sheet in relation to the $125M it drew as part of the Pharmakon Loan Agreement. GERN's net cash used in operating activities was $111M in 2025, meaning at the current rate, and taking out GERN's noncurrent debt, GERN could keep operating for ~2.5 years, or into mid-2028. In reality, we will soon find out (perhaps in the next couple of quarters) if GERN is going to grow Rytelo beyond $47M-$49M in sales a quarter, at which point the company could be on the road to cash flow positivity well before its cash dwindles to concerning levels.

GERN trades currently with a market cap of $992.8M ($1.55 per share).

Risks

There are several risks to holding GERN long, a few of which will be considered in this section.

Firstly, GERN could report particularly modest Q1 '26 sales growth, or no growth at all, leading to the market questioning if the company can meet its 2026 guidance.

Further, GERN could report delays in reporting results from its clinical studies, which could trigger those trading around those readouts to exit their position. GERN could also report disappointing results from its trials, which would also likely trigger a fall in the stock.

Lastly, GERN could see further delays in the European launch of Rytelo for LR-MDS. The company already has approval, and is in the process of ensuring reimbursement.

So what we're doing currently is working and engaging with the HTAs, the health technology assessment processes around Europe because we do have an EMA approval, which is very good. But as you know, in Europe, it's -- that tells you that it's safe and effective. Somebody still needs to pay for it and at what price and at what comparison; so we're doing that work anyway regardless of if it's us who's going to market it or if we partner with somebody else. All these options are all on the table.

Harout Semerjian, CEO, President & Director at GERN, Barclays Conference, March 10, 2026.

Disappointing outcomes from that work, such as a low price of reimbursement, delays, or the announcement of a European partnership on unfavorable terms, could all cause GERN's stock to sell off.

Conclusions

There is no real sales growth to speak of; GERN has been posting net product revenues of about $47M-$49M for about five quarters now. Yet the company reiterated its guidance of net product revenues for 2026 of $220M-$240M. For those that tuned into or read the conference call transcript, the CEO has suggested that IQVIA data and other data for Q1 '26 appear to show the company is justified in reiterating their 2026 guidance. Further, the company's IMpactMF study could reach an interim in H2 '26, meaning a run-up could be in play heading forward. Beyond IMpactMF, there could be additional readouts from the company's trials, including the IST, which provide further shots on goal for the company.

Since a readout from IMpactMF represents a major opportunity for GERN to double its revenues, even without Rytelo growing in the current indication, and the phase 2 data suggesting the drug could perform, I believe further run-up in GERN's stock is possible. Further, the valuation remains passable, with GERN trading with an EV/Sales of 4.14 based on 2025 sales (the first full year of Rytelo sales), so further run-up wouldn't require ridiculous valuations even considering current sales. Considering these factors, I again rate GERN a Buy.

data.title: BBB Foods: Trade-Down Winner
data.description:
Just like me when I visited Cancun for the first time (almost ten years ago, how time flies!), BBB Foods (TBBB) is having its first hangover in at least a year.

And, unlike one or two tequilas—yes, I'm weak with alcohol—the problem here was the valuation.

I already told you in my last article on this hard-discounter that maybe they had gone too far. I argued with Wall St. (which maintained its 'Buy' rating) and said that the stock offered little or almost no upside near $35.

Time passed, and it seems that this has aged well.

BBB Foods did go above ~$35, but soon returned to trading within the fairly valued range and has been there for some time.

But you know how hangovers are, don't you? They can last a few hours or ruin your whole day.

And, in the case of Tiendas 3B, a pullback might even be healthy for long-term holders to add shares here and there. The problem is trying to answer whether ~20x EBITDA is enough to pay for this gem.

It continues to grow, and very quickly. But if you pay for perfection, reality may eventually come knocking.

In my last article about Betterware (BWMX), I told you that things aren't 'muy buenas' on the other side of the border.

Blame the CJNG, inflation, or low confidence (both consumer and business). Everything seems to point to folks not opening their wallets much, at least in the first half of the year.

This is terrible for discretionary items, by the way.

What many forget is that Tiendas 3B is a hard-discounter, and if there's a time to gain market share, that time seems to be now.

Why a ‘Bad’ Macro Isn’t So Bad for Tiendas 3B

Mexican consumers are making the classic trade-down when expectations are low.

It's basically the same thing you and I do when we can't spend as much on groceries—we visit hard-discounters (Aldi, Lidl if you live in the U.S., or No Frills if you live in Canada).

If you read my articles on other grocery stores, you'll notice that even traditional supermarkets are putting up discounter banners to compete. And it's precisely these that are stealing market share.

Here is a comparison of their same-store sales with ANTAD:

You don't see same-store sales of 16.6% every day. But I need to break it down for you.

  1. Average check increased by 11%
  2. Traffic increased by only 2.5%
  3. Penetration of private label brands increased by 400 bp to 58%

I've talked a lot about the dangers of check-driven growth. But here it's a little different.

I see that many Mexicans who previously viewed Tiendas 3B as a kind of convenience store due to the low SKUs and small size are now visiting them more frequently, and when they do, they're buying more things.

CEO Hatoum said this didn't come from price increases, but rather from a better product mix.

Average ticket size increased by 11%, driven primarily by items per ticket and an improved product mix and to a much lesser extent by price inflation.

And with the shift towards private labels that I showed you in the first article, this not only helps margins but also the average check when they target expensive products (ice cream is a great example of this).

In a scenario where the 'Kingpin Effect' accelerates further, they have the advantage of expanding from clusters, and DCs are at most 150 km apart.

Here they could have some advantage over local chains in case there are more road blockages in the area. Even nearby stores can share SKUs without problems.

Modeling FY 2026

Tiendas 3B performed very well in Q4 FY 2025. But you and I (probably, I think) already expected that.

Same-store sales surged 16.6% once again. And, despite lagging behind the entire FY 2025 (18.3%), I think that's more due to difficult comparisons.

As you saw before, transactions are growing in the low single digits.

Any stronger gains going forward need to come from the average check. I imagine that in H1 2026 they'll start to see some acceleration in transactions, but that won't move the needle that much.

Openings are accelerating as well. In the entire FY 2025, they opened 574 stores and are now sitting at 3,346 across Mexico.

If you ask me, all of this already seems to be priced into the market at this point.

But is FY 2026 also priced in? Well, it's always difficult to say, but I have a few hunches here.

The most obvious is the slowdown in same-store sales. Guidance targets something between 13% and 16%, falling more than 200 bp in the best-case scenario. They will likely compensate for this by opening 590 to 630 stores (3,956 stores by the end of the year if I use the midpoint).

By the end of the year, this should lead them to report revenue of MXN 100.8 to 103.1 billion. This is a slowdown from the ~36% growth of the entire FY 2025.

Even so, this seems to have disappointed Wall Street somewhat.

It appears they were reporting MXN 105.3 billion in revenue before the results came out and are still targeting slightly above the midpoint of their guidance at MXN 103.5 billion.

The EBITDA margin excluding SBC was 5.6% for the entire FY 2025, slightly above the 5-year average of 5.2%. As I expect food inflation to continue rising and Tiendas to absorb some of that, at least in H1 2026, I will use this 5.2% for FY 2026.

This would give me something close to MXN 5.3 billion in EBITDA ($296.9 million converting to US dollars).

So, by my calculations, the stock is trading at almost 15x EBITDA excluding SBC. If I consider SBC, we would end up with ~19x.

Does Anything Change in the Valuation?

My previous range for BBB Foods' revenue in FY 2030 was between $11.5 and $13.5 billion, with $12.5 billion in the base case.

I don't know if it's because there are few estimates out there, but it seems that this has increased to $12.4 to $16.2 billion. I don't think that's realistic.

I think it's better to go back a year and aim for between $10.5 and $12.9 billion.

With the same EBITDA margin range of 5 to 7% (I was expecting maturity in FY 2030 at ~8%), they could reach something between $525 and $903 million.

Plugging that into a mature grocery multiple between 6 and 8x and using a discount rate of ~7%, I found a midpoint price target of ~$31.70.

Using the same model for FY 2030 with the Wall Street estimates, I arrive at something close to $42.90. So, even in the most optimistic estimates for the stock, it could give an upside of almost 30% from here.

And that's without considering SBC.

The midpoint of these two projections is very close to my $35 from the last article.

Another way to think about it here is by looking at the P/CF.

If I take the projected midpoint EBITDA for FY 2029 at $714 million and assume a normalized EBITDA-to-OCF conversion rate of ~70%, I arrive at an OCF of $503.3 million. Peers like Jerónimo Martins (JRONY) trade at ~9x OCF per share. This would give me an OCF per share of $4.40 and a price target of ~$32.30, discounting that to present value at 7%.

An upside of ~5% in my base case is not what I'm looking for in a growth stock.

I'll maintain my 'Hold' rating here. I think Tiendas 3B will handle the Mexican environment well and gain some market share.

At $25 to $30 it could be a good entry point if you're aiming for the long term.

data.title: Axon Enterprise: Exceptional Growth Already Priced In
data.description:
Where Execution Meets Valuation

Axon Enterprise (AXON) grew revenue 33% in 2025, delivered eight consecutive quarters of 30%+ top-line growth, and exited the year with $14.4B in future contracted bookings. By almost any operational measure, this is one of the best-executing companies in the public markets. The stock, however, has fallen ~43% from its August 2025 all-time high of ~$886 to roughly $506 today, and investors are wondering whether that correction has finally created an entry point.

I think the answer is not yet. The operational story is compelling, and I believe the business itself has a genuinely durable competitive position in public safety technology. The problem is the math at the current price. At ~$506, AXON trades at roughly 74x 2026 consensus non-GAAP EPS of $6.85. That multiple prices in near-perfect execution of management’s $6B 2028 revenue target and continued margin expansion, while leaving almost no room for disappointment. The valuation grade from Seeking Alpha’s quant system is a D, with nearly every metric rated F relative to sector medians.

Along with these grades comes a hold rating by Seeking Alpha’s quant model, that only just recently went from sell to hold following AXON’s Q4 earnings.

What makes the valuation particularly difficult to justify at current levels is the stock-based compensation picture. AXON recorded $610M in SBC in 2025, representing ~22% of revenue. That expense is the primary reason GAAP net income fell from $377M to $125M despite revenue growing by $697M.

Adjusted EBITDA of $710M and non-GAAP EPS of $6.85 strip out this cost entirely, and I believe investors should be cautious about building a valuation case on metrics which exclude an expense of this magnitude.

Based on my analysis , I rate AXON a Hold with a fair value range of $450 to $550, reflecting roughly 58x to 80x forward non-GAAP earnings. The business deserves a premium multiple for its growth profile, competitive moat, and AI-driven product cycle. But at the current price, I think the risk-reward favours patience over entry.

Axon Q4 2025 Earnings and Catalysts

The Q4 results were unambiguously strong in my opinion. Revenue of $796.7M beat consensus of $755.3M by 5.5%, and adjusted EPS of $2.15 crushed the $1.60 estimate. Full-year revenue reached $2.78B, up 33% YoY, with a 3-year CAGR also at 33%. The growth is broad-based: Software & Services revenue grew >40%, hardware revenue grew >30% driven by Platform Solutions, and international bookings exceeded $1B for the first time, up >50% YoY.

The bookings trajectory deserves special attention, as annual bookings of $7.4B were up 46% YoY, and management noted on the earnings call that fourth-quarter bookings alone nearly matched full-year bookings from just two years ago. The $14.4B backlog provides roughly five years of revenue visibility at the current run rate, which is unusual depth for a company in this sector.

Net revenue retention accelerated to 125%, meaning existing customers are spending 25% more annually before any new logos are added. I also think the >$750M in AI-specific bookings is a significant proof point for the AI Era Plan, which bundles Draft One, Axon Assistant, and other AI tools into premium subscription tiers.

On the margin front, the picture is more nuanced than the headline numbers suggest. Adjusted EBITDA margin expanded to 25.5%, 50 bps above AXON’s own target, and adjusted gross margin held at 63%. But this is where the impressiveness stops, as GAAP profitability tells a different story.

GAAP net income of $125M on $2.78B in revenue produces a 4.5% net margin, down from 18% in 2024. Not great. The entire margin compression is driven by non-cash items, primarily $610M in SBC (up from $383M in 2024) and $94M in interest expense related to convertible note issuances. The SBC figure is particularly notable because it grew 60% YoY while revenue grew 33%, meaning compensation is inflating faster than the top line. Management has guided for annual dilution from SBC to stay below 2.5% going forward, which I think is an acknowledgment that the 2025 run rate was unsustainable.

AXON Valuation and Price Target

The core valuation problem is that AXON’s multiple already assumes the growth story plays out, when we still haven’t seen it come fully to fruition. At a forward P/E (non-GAAP) of 65x versus a sector median of ~19x, the market is paying a 240% premium for Axon’s earnings stream. That premium has to be earned by sustained 30%+ growth, margin expansion, and a normalisation of the SBC burden, all simultaneously. If any one of those metrics disappoints, the risk of repricing could be severe and we could see AXON be punished.

On a trailing GAAP basis the P/E exceeds 300x, which illustrates just how far non-GAAP adjustments are carrying the valuation narrative. The only metric which appears to partially justify the multiple is the PEG ratio (non-GAAP FWD) of 1.68, roughly in line with the sector median, but that ratio only works if you accept the ~39% long-term EPS CAGR as sustainable, which again requires the SBC to shrink materially, otherwise I just do not see it happen.

The growth metrics are where I think the thesis around AXON becomes a little bit complicated. Revenue growth (YoY) of 33.5% and a forward EPS CAGR of 38.8% both earn A or A+ grades, placing AXON in the top tier of growth companies in the sector. But the market is already paying for that growth in full, as we already discussed. The gap between the A growth grade and the D valuation grade is the entire hold thesis. Exceptional businesses can still be poor investments at the wrong price, and I believe AXON sits just on the edge of that.

Consensus estimates project non-GAAP EPS of $7.75 for 2026 and $9.77 for 2027, and with the current price of ~$505 at the time of wriiting, the stock trades at ~65x 2026E and ~52x 2027E earnings. For context, the mean analyst price target is ~$786, implying roughly 55% upside. I think the analyst consensus is correct directionally, but achievability over a 12-month horizon depends heavily on broader market conditions for high-multiple growth stocks, and with the uncertainty we have in the market now, I am starting to wonder if we will see that.

My own fair value range of $450 to $550 is anchored to a 58x to 72x multiple on 2026E non-GAAP EPS of $7.75. The lower end reflects a scenario where tech multiples continue compressing and the SBC burden weighs on sentiment, while the upper assumes flawless execution and the AI product cycle accelerates faster than expected.

Forward Outlook for Axon

Management’s 2026 guidance set revenue around $3.53B to $3.61B, representing 27% to 30% growth YoY, which came in above the $3.44B consensus at the time of the report. Adjusted EBITDA margin is targeted at 25.5%, flat with 2025. The longer-term 2028 target of $6B in revenue with 28% adjusted EBITDA margins implies a ~29% CAGR from the 2025 base, and management has committed to keeping annual SBC dilution below 2.5% and converting 60% of adjusted EBITDA to free cash flow on average.

I think the $6B target is achievable but the path requires sustained 29% annual growth for three years straight, which means no material deceleration relative to current levels. With the $14.4B contracted backlog providing a floor, ARR of $1.35B growing at 35% anchors the recurring revenue stream, and new product categories such as Dedrone (counter-drone), Axon Air, and the 911-to-Closure strategy expands the addressable market.

The AI product cycle is the most meaningful near-term catalyst in my eyes. Draft One, which automates police report generation from body camera footage, has processed over 100K reports and is reportedly saving agencies 50-80% of administrative time. Axon Assistant had 200K monthly messages by year-end.

The >$750M in AI bookings that AXON had in 2025 is a strong starting point, and the premium subscription plans which bundle these tools are now adopted by ~30% of customers, and up from a much smaller base a year ago. Each premium subscription upgrade increases per-seat revenue significantly, which is what drives the 125% NRR figure.

Risk to the outlook is primarily margin-related, as adjusted gross margin ticked down to 63% from 63.2% in 2024, pressured by tariffs (which are virtually gone now) on hardware components and the mix shift toward Platform Solutions, which carry lower margins than the legacy body camera and TASER segments. If tariff costs escalate beyond the ~15% global rate Axon has embedded in guidance, margin expansion toward the 28% EBITDA target becomes harder to deliver.

Quantitative Analysis

The options and dark pool data add useful short-term context to the fundamental picture that I just took you through, though I should note that the options positioning for AXON is not telling a strong directional story at the moment. It essentially just adds to the hold thesis.

The most actionable signal comes from FINRA’s dark pool data. Dark pools are private trading venues where institutional investors execute large orders over the counter, as to not disrupt general market trading. The buy ratio measures how many shares institutions are buying versus selling through these venues, with a reading of 1.0 indicating equal buying and selling.

The chart above shows something incredibly interesting in my opinion. Between late December 2025 and mid-January 2026, when AXON was trading in the $600 to $650 range, dark pool buy ratios spiked dramatically, reaching buy ratios of 3x to 5x on several days. This is aggressive institutional accumulation at those price levels, and at prices well above current spot.

Since the late January selloff, however, the 10-day EMA buy ratio has rolled over to approximately 1.4, and the most recent readings cluster near or below the neutral line. My point here is that institutional buyers were active at higher prices but have not yet returned with conviction at the current ~$506 level. This is not necessarily bearish, as it may simply reflect patience, but it does suggest that the stock lacks a near-term institutional at the moment.

Gamma exposure (GEX) measures the hedging obligations of options market makers, and it can reveal price levels where the stock is likely to encounter support or resistance due to dealer positioning. For readers unfamiliar with this concept: when market makers sell options at a particular strike price, they must hedge by buying or selling shares (and vice versa). The aggregate of these hedging flows can pin or repel the stock at certain levels, and strikes with high GEX concentrations usually become price magnets. .

The GEX structure shows the stock sitting below its zero GEX line at $550, which means AXON is currently in negative gamma territory, and in practical terms, this means dealer hedging tends to amplify price moves in both directions rather than dampening them. Think of it as a high volatility regime. The put support level at $520 and the call wall at $470 create a wide trading range. This is consistent with the elevated volatility AXON has experienced since the January correction, and suggests that the stock could continue to see big moves around catalysts such as earnings or broader market shifts.

Open interest distribution confirms this picture, with the heaviest concentration of put contracts sitting at the $450 and $500 strikes, while the largest call clusters are at $500, $570, and $600.

The options data, taken all together, paints a picture of elevated uncertainty without a clear consensus on direction. The negative GEX environment means volatility is likely to remain elevated, with dark pool data suggesting patience rather than urgency among institutional participants. I think the lack of directional conviction is a key signal to not add any shares at the moment, as we could be a collapse in price just as well as we could see a move up. It is just too unclear for me, which way AXON goes from here.

Risks and Concerns

There are a few risks to my hold-thesis, and the primary risk is valuation compression. At 65x to 74x forward earnings, any miss on growth or margins could trigger a sharp repricing. We actually saw this happen after their Q3 earnings, as the stock fell 17% after the Q3 2025 results when EPS of $1.17 missed the $1.54 consensus, showing just how little room the multiple leaves for execution gaps.

SBC is the second major concern in my view. The $610M expense in 2025 represented 22% of revenue and grew faster than the top line. While management has guided for <2.5% annual dilution going forward, the absolute dollar amount of SBC could continue rising as Axon scales, and any reacceleration in share-based grants would erode the GAAP earnings improvement investors expect.

Government budget risk is structural and ever-present, since AXON’s customer base is overwhelmingly public sector, and while the $14.4B backlog provides multi-year contracts, those contracts typically include non-appropriation clauses which allow agencies to cancel if funding is not renewed. A fiscal tightening cycle at the state and local level, or a change in federal grant programs, could slow new bookings growth even if existing contracts remain intact.

Despite these risks, I think the underlying business quality is high enough that a material deterioration in the fundamental story is unlikely over the medium term. The issue is not whether Axon is a good business; it is whether the current price adequately compensates for the risks that exist, and whether the market is going to support a move up.

Conclusion

AXON is executing at a great level, with a 33% revenue growth, $14.4B backlog, 125% NRR, and AI-driven product cycle are all best-in-class metrics. The 2028 target of $6B in revenue should be achievable given the trajectory, but at ~$506 and 65x to 74x forward earnings, the market prices in the optimistic scenario with little margin for failure. The SBC burden of $610M, which causes GAAP net income to dramatically lag non-GAAP metrics, adds a layer of risk the market may not be fully appreciating.

I rate AXON a Hold. The business deserves to be on every growth investor’s watchlist, and a pullback toward the lower end of the $400 to $450 range would, I believe, create a more compelling entry point. At the current price, patience appears the better strategy.

data.title: PCEF: Understanding The Structure And Suitability Of This CEF ETF
data.description:
The Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF (PCEF) is an exchange-traded index fund that aims to track the price and yield performance of the S-Network Composite Closed-End Fund Index. As the name of the index suggests, this is an index that consists of closed-end funds that manage assets on behalf of investors in an attempt to obtain money that is used to pay a distribution to investors. As might be expected based on this description, the index will generally have one of the highest yields of any asset available in the American markets. The Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF invests its assets into the securities that comprise this index, so naturally it receives the distributions that they pay out. PCEF then distributes this money to its own investors, after deducting its expenses from the total. As is the case with most Invesco funds, the Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF has a very reasonable expense ratio, so this results in PCEF itself having one of the highest yields that can be obtained from an exchange-traded fund in the American markets. This could make the fund a reasonably attractive investment for an investor who is seeking to earn a high level of income from the assets in their portfolio.

About The Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF

As just stated in the introduction, the Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF is an exchange-traded index fund that aims to track the price and yield performance of the S-Network Composite Closed-End Fund Index. The fund’s website offers the following description:

The Invesco CEF Composite ETF is based on the S-Network Composite Closed-End Fund Index. The Fund will normally invest at least 90% of its total assets in securities of funds included in the Index. The Fund is a “fund-of-funds,” as it invests its assets in the common shares of funds included in the Index rather than in individual securities. The Index currently includes closed-end funds that invest in taxable investment grade fixed-income securities, taxable high yield fixed-income securities and others that utilize an equity option writing (selling) strategy. The Fund and the Index are rebalanced and reconstituted quarterly.

As stated in the description, the Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF is a fund of funds. This is a designation that has long been used in the hedge fund world, but it is much less common to see such a fund trading in the public markets. Investopedia offers a description of a fund of funds:

A fund of funds is one that, instead of investing in a pool of securities like stocks and bonds, buys shares in other funds. These “multi-manager” investments offer investors further diversification and access to the expertise of other skilled fund managers.

In short, the Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF does not invest in common equities, preferred stocks, or bonds like most other exchange-traded funds in the market. Rather, it invests in the common equity shares of closed-end funds that own these assets. As Investopedia points out, this could offer investors a diversification advantage. Rather than purchasing a single closed-end fund and relying on the performance of that fund’s individual manager as a source of investment returns, PCEF provides investors with a way to put their money into many different funds at the same time.
